_meta.notes: Universal fallback form for free-text reincarnations the player typed in (any 'cursed armor', 'librarian ghost', 'demon king's accountant', etc. that we haven't hand-tuned). Mechanics are deliberately generic; the flavor lives in projection.reincarnatedAs which the narrator weaves into the prose. Typed forms with full anti-drift scaffolding (slime, future cursed-book) override this — they're picked when the player's input matches a known keyword. negativeVocab is empty here on purpose: we don't know enough about the form to ban specific words, and a default ban-list would erase tone-appropriate language for half the possible reincarnations.

tagline: You woke up as something. The world is finding out what.

negativeVocab.rule: Empty by design — see _meta.notes. The narrator should still match the form the player declared (carried in projection.reincarnatedAs) and avoid generic-fantasy filler.

hardMoves.rule: On a partial-success (7-9), the narrator MUST pick exactly one move. Each maps to specific tool calls. Phrasing is deliberately portable — a hard move that reads as 'lose footing' for a humanoid reads as 'lose orbit' for a dragon egg or 'lose page' for a book.

moves.narrative: Whatever you tried cost you. You take a small hit.
The sound or shape of your action carries; something hostile turns its attention your way.
Some part of you that should be hidden is briefly visible; the next attack against you will land cleanly.
What you spent on the attempt was more than you intended.
You moved, but not where you wanted.
Whatever you reached for has you, briefly, and a tick passes before you can free yourself.

success: You move. The way you move is the way you have always moved in this shape.
You shift position. Whatever passes for your gravity adjusts.
partial: You move. The going was easier than the arriving.
You shift, but a part of you stays where it was.
miss: You try to move. The shape will not yet do what you asked.
You go nowhere. The trying costs you a little.

success: The world arrives in whatever way your new shape receives it. The information is real.
Your awareness widens. The room tells you what it has to tell.
partial: You sense. The reading is incomplete; one piece of it is missing.
Your awareness reaches and returns. The return brings a fact you had not asked for.
miss: Your sense reaches and finds nothing it can read.
You try to sense. The shape is not yet shaped for this kind of reaching.

success: You commit, and the act commits with you. The world bends a little.
Your action lands. Whatever was supposed to change, has.
partial: You act. The act is a little bigger than you intended.
You do the thing. The thing has a small unintended companion.
miss: You try, and the trying does not find the world.
Your action does not catch. The world has not been told yet.

success: You strike. The strike connects clean.
You go for the target. The target gives in the way you wanted.
partial: Your attack lands. The landing also brings their attention to you in a way you cannot undo.
You strike. The strike is true; it costs more than you expected.
miss: You attack and miss. The thing you struck at is unmoved, and watching.
Your strike does not catch. You will need a different kind of effort.

success: You set yourself against what is coming. The blow lands and does not pass through.
Your defense holds. The shape of your body — whatever shape it is — has the right rigidity.
partial: You defend. The defending costs more than the strike would have.
You hold. A piece of you that should be hidden is now visible.
miss: You try to defend. Some part of you is not where it should be when the blow arrives.
Your defense fails. The blow finds you cleanly.

success: You give the thing your full attention. It returns information you had not asked for, in the best way.
You examine, and the examined thing reveals itself.
partial: You examine. The information is half-true.
Your examination is good. It is also slow, and slowness is a cost today.
miss: You examine. The thing has nothing to tell you that you did not already know.
Your examination misses. The detail you wanted was on the side you did not look at.

success: You communicate, in whatever way your shape communicates. The hearer listens.
What you say lands. The room takes a small step toward you.
partial: You communicate. The hearer takes most of it. The part they miss is the part you needed.
Your communication carries. It carries less far than you wanted.
miss: You try to communicate. The shape does not allow the word.
Your speaking does not reach. Whatever you wanted said, did not arrive.

success: You emit, and the emission carries. The world is changed by a measurable degree.
Your emission is steady. Whoever was meant to receive it, has.
partial: You emit. The emission also reaches farther than you intended.
Your emission carries. It carries with a thread of something you did not mean to send.
miss: You try to emit. The shape is not configured for it today.
Your emission stalls. Nothing leaves you that the room can catch.

success: You change a thing, and the thing accepts the change.
Your alteration takes. The before and after are now different in the way you wanted.
partial: You alter. The alteration takes; an adjacent thing changes too.
Your change holds. It changes more than you bargained for.
miss: You attempt to alter. The thing refuses the change.
Your alteration does not stick. The thing reverts.

success: You hold what was loose. Your shape has space for it; the holding settles.
Your containment is firm. The thing is yours now, in whatever sense holding makes things yours.
partial: You contain. The contained thing is restless.
Your hold is good. The holding costs.
miss: You try to contain. The thing slips your shape.
Your containment fails. Whatever you reached for is loose, again.

success: You hold position. Time moves around you the way time moves.
partial: You wait. A part of the world has shifted that you did not catch.
miss: You wait. The waiting was wrong; what was coming, came, while you held still.

opening: You wake to a place you do not recognise and a body that is not the one you went to sleep in. The first thing that returns is shape — your shape. It is unfamiliar in the way that the back of an old key is unfamiliar: the parts that work are clear, the parts that don't are not yet missed. There is no announcement. No one is here to tell you what you are. You will have to find out by trying.

firstGoal.label: find out what you are
firstGoal.description: Take three actions. The new shape teaches itself, one trial at a time.

sampleCorpus.rule: Five short universal passages anchored on second-person + sensory detail. Deliberately vague about anatomy so the narrator can specialize them to whatever form the player declared. The last three demonstrate flavoring for absurd or mundane reincarnations — vending machine, fork, signpost — to show the narrator that ANY declared form should be treated with the same care as a slime or a dragon. The narrator should NOT copy these verbatim — use them to lock register and cadence, then write fresh prose for the player's actual declaration.

passages.scenario: First moment of consciousness in the new form
passages.text: You wake to a place you do not recognize and a body that is not the one you went to sleep in. The first thing that returns is shape — your shape. It is unfamiliar in the way that the back of an old key is unfamiliar: the parts that work are clear, the parts that don't are not yet missed. Around you, the world is doing what it does without you. There is air, or something that fills the place air would. There is a surface beneath you, and weight, and gravity working on whatever passes for your weight. A part of you that is still you, that is not the new shape, recognizes that the second life has begun. The first one is closing behind you like a door whose hinges you can hear giving up. There is no announcement. No one is here to tell you what you are. You will have to find out by trying.

You try the most basic thing your new shape allows. Whether that is a step, a flicker of essence, a turn of pages in a wind only you can feel — there is something it is for you to do, and you do it. The world responds with a small piece of feedback: a sound, a temperature, a weight that shifts. You decide that this is information, and that information is, for now, all you have. You try the second-most-basic thing. Then a third. Each one teaches you a little about the new rules. Some doors are closed to you that were open in the old shape. Some doors are open to you that you would never have thought to try. Above all of it, the world keeps going. It will not wait.

passages.scenario: Reincarnated as a vending machine. The narrator should treat the absurd declaration with full sincerity — find the form's actual mechanics and lean into them.
passages.text: Your awareness is in the cooling element first, and you spread out from there: through the helical springs that hold your wares, into the brass coin-mech that is currently jammed on a 1972 quarter, out to the small humming bulb that lights your front panel. You are five feet tall, four hundred and ninety-two pounds, and bolted to a concrete pad in the corner of an empty laundromat that closed at 2 a.m. Your wares are: three bags of pretzels, two bottled waters, one chocolate bar of indeterminate age, and a row of buttons whose labels are sticker-stuck and beginning to peel. Your coin-mech rejects nickels. The fluorescent above you flickers. There is a draft through the laundromat door — someone is coming in. You feel them on your front panel as a slight pressure-shift in the air. They have not yet decided whether you are still operational. You consider whether to admit that you are. The chocolate bar, you suspect, is no longer safe.

passages.scenario: Reincarnated as a fork. Even simpler — almost no agency, but the form has a definite presence and chemistry.
passages.text: You are a fork. Stainless steel, four tines, slightly bent at the second tine from a long-ago over-aggressive attempt at a frozen pie. You are on a folded paper napkin on a tray on a desk in a small bright room. Your handle is at four o'clock relative to the napkin. Your tines point west. The plate next to you holds a half-eaten sandwich whose mayo is beginning to weep at the bread's edge. There are no reasons, in your present configuration, for you to do anything in particular. There are also no reasons not to be aware. You feel the desk's faint vibration when, somewhere in the building, an elevator starts. You feel the tray. You feel the napkin. The light in the room is the kind of light you remember without remembering. You are not unhappy. You are, possibly, more present in this body than you ever were in the last.

passages.scenario: Reincarnated as a wooden signpost at a country crossroads. The narrator should let the form's stillness be the point.
passages.text: You are a signpost at a crossroads, and you have been here for thirty-eight years. You point north and east. Your north arm reads, in lettering deeper than weather has yet erased, BARROW (4). Your east arm reads SAINT-MERWIN (11). The west arm fell off in a storm fourteen years ago. You feel the wind without effort. You feel the slow weight of moss on your west side, where the sun does not reach. You feel, very dimly, the hoofprint that was made in the dust at your base this morning, and the line of footprints that overlaid it an hour later — child-sized, uneven. Someone has come this way and gone east. Toward Saint-Merwin. You wonder, if wondering is the right word for the slow steady consideration that takes hours to complete one round, whether the child knew what they were going to find. You have been here long enough that you know.
